core.intro: Figs are one of the most rewarding and forgiving fruit trees for Australian gardens: a Mediterranean crop that wants full sun, free-draining soil and a hot dry summer to ripen. The state sections cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes below apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Almost every fig sold in Australia is a common (parthenocarpic) variety, so the choice comes down to flavour, colour and what you want to make rather than pollination. Dark-skinned figs include Black Genoa (large, rich and a reliable heavy cropper, a good all-rounder), Brown Turkey (vigorous, hardy and the usual first fig for a new gardener) and Preston Prolific (a Victorian variety with thick, sweet, creamy flesh). Green and amber-skinned figs include White Adriatic (the classic drying fig), White Genoa (tender, well-suited to cooler districts) and Excel (very sweet and resistant to splitting). For a pot or a small courtyard, dwarf types such as Sweet Temptation stay compact. Decide whether you want figs for fresh eating, drying or jam, then match a named variety from the in-stock table above.

sections.heading: Pollination: do you need two trees?
sections.body: No. The common figs that Australian nurseries sell are self-sufficient: they set a full crop on a single tree with no pollination and no fig wasp, so one tree is all you need. The famous fig and wasp partnership only matters for Smyrna and San Pedro figs, which must be pollinated through caprifigs (a process called caprification) and are not sold in the general trade; they were once grown in older South Australian districts where the wasp was established. A handful of specialty types, such as the variety Spanish Dessert, still need a pollinator, but every mainstream variety fruits on its own.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Figs want full sun and free-draining soil. They are famously unfussy, tolerating poor, sandy and mildly alkaline soils that defeat other fruit trees, but they will not accept wet feet, so avoid spots where water pools and plant on a low mound in heavy ground. Give them room: fig roots are vigorous and travel a long way near the surface, so keep a tree well clear of drains, paving and house foundations. In a small garden you can turn that vigour to your advantage by growing the tree in a large pot or a root-restricted bed, which curbs leafy growth and brings on fruit. Container-grown plants can go in for most of the year as long as you water them through summer (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Figs are drought-hardy once established, but a tree carrying fruit needs steady moisture while the figs are swelling: let it dry out and the crop drops, then a sudden drink as the fruit ripens makes the skins split. Water deeply and regularly through the dry months, then ease off as the fruit colours up. Go easy on nitrogen, which pushes soft leafy growth at the expense of figs, so a light feed in late winter or early spring is plenty.

sections.heading: Pruning and the two crops
sections.body: Figs can carry two crops. A light early (breba) crop forms on the tips of last year's wood and ripens in early summer, then the main crop sets on the current season's new growth and ripens from late summer into autumn. Prune in winter while the tree is bare: hard pruning removes the breba wood but drives strong new growth for a heavy main crop, and figs tolerate being cut back hard. Renewal pruning, where you cycle out the oldest trunk each winter and let a fresh shoot replace it, keeps a vigorous fig to about head height while it keeps fruiting on young wood.

sections.heading: Harvest and ripening
sections.body: Figs do not ripen once they are picked, so they must be left to ripen fully on the tree. A ripe fig softens, its neck bends so the fruit hangs down, the skin may split a little and a bead of nectar can form at the eye. Pick gently every day or two through the season, because ripe figs bruise easily and do not keep more than a couple of days. The milky sap from the stalk of a just-picked or pruned fig can irritate skin and lips, so wear gloves if you are handling a lot of fruit.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, cutting-grown tree rather than a seedling. Figs strike easily from dormant hardwood cuttings and come true to type that way, whereas seed-grown figs are a gamble and can turn out to be inedible wild (caprifig) types. Deciduous figs are sold bare-root through the dormant season, roughly May to August, often more cheaply than potted stock, while potted plants are available year round. Check the label for the variety name and use the in-stock table above to see what is available now and what it costs.

faqs.q: Do I need two fig trees to get fruit?
faqs.a: No. The common figs sold by Australian nurseries are self-fertile and set a full crop on a single tree with no pollination and no fig wasp. Only a few specialty types, such as Smyrna figs and the variety Spanish Dessert, need a pollinator, and these are rarely sold in the general trade.

faqs.q: How long until a fig tree fruits?
faqs.a: Figs are quick. A cutting-grown named variety often carries a few fruit in its first or second year and crops well within three years, faster than most fruit trees. Restricting the roots in a pot can bring fruiting on sooner still.

faqs.q: Why won't my figs ripen after I pick them?
faqs.a: Figs do not ripen once they are off the tree, so a fig picked early stays hard and bland. Leave each fruit until it softens, droops on its stalk and smells sweet, then eat or use it within a day or two because ripe figs do not keep.

faqs.q: Will a fig tree's roots damage pipes or paving?
faqs.a: They can. Fig roots are vigorous and spread widely near the surface, so plant a tree several metres from drains, paths and foundations, or grow it in a large pot or a root-restricted bed to keep it in check (which also encourages fruiting).

WA.intro: Growing figs in Western Australia. The south-west has the hot dry summers and mild wet winters of a Mediterranean climate, which is close to ideal for figs, and they have been grown in Perth gardens and the Swan Valley since the early settlers.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Figs thrive in a Mediterranean climate like Perth's, with cool winters and a long, hot, dry summer to ripen the fruit, and they need only the low winter chill that the south-west supplies easily. The dry summer air is a real advantage: it keeps the fungal diseases that plague figs in humid regions to a minimum. Hot inland and northern districts can grow figs too, as long as the trees are irrigated through summer.

sections.heading: Where figs grow in WA
sections.body: The Swan Valley on Perth's edge is the best-known fig district, with fruit ripening through the warmer months, and figs crop well right across the Perth metro area and up into the Perth Hills. The cooler, wetter South West can grow them too, though ripening comes later there and disease pressure is a little higher.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA's hot summers ripen figs early. Expect a light breba crop in early summer and the main crop from about January into autumn, picked tree-ripe every day or two.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: Mediterranean fruit fly is established across much of WA and ripe figs are a high-susceptibility host, so net or bait trees as the fruit softens. Birds are the other big problem and often need netting. The upside is the dry summer: fig rust, which needs long spells of leaf wetness, is far less troublesome here than in the humid eastern states.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's strict quarantine means live plants from other states must be permitted on the WA Organism List and are usually certified and treated before entry, so many eastern nurseries will not post fig trees here. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship to WA.

faqs.q: When do you harvest figs in WA?
faqs.a: From about January through autumn for the main crop, with a lighter breba crop in early summer. Perth's hot, dry summers ripen figs earlier than the cooler southern and eastern states.

faqs.q: Why won't some nurseries post fig trees to WA?
faqs.a: WA quarantine law requires live plants to be permitted on the WA Organism List and usually certified and treated before they cross the border, so many eastern-states nurseries choose not to ship plants here. The table above shows sellers that can.

QLD.intro: Growing figs in Queensland. Figs want a dry Mediterranean climate, so in Queensland they do best in the cooler, drier inland and the elevated south, not the humid coast or the tropics.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Queensland's warm, moist, humid summers are out of a fig's comfort zone: the tree grows happily but the humidity brings fig rust and makes ripening fruit split, and disease pressure is worst on the subtropical coast. Figs do far better west of the dividing range and in the cooler, drier elevated south. On the humid coast, choose tougher varieties, keep the canopy open and airy, and expect to manage rust.

sections.heading: Where figs grow in QLD
sections.body: Commercial figs are grown in the Lockyer Valley west of Brisbane, where growers report fruit fly as their main battle, and in the drier, elevated Granite Belt around Stanthorpe. Closer to the coast, orchards in the Brisbane hinterland around Morayfield still crop well, but they have to spray for rust through the wet season.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Warm lowland districts ripen early, with a breba crop from late spring and the main crop through summer; cooler elevated areas such as the Granite Belt run several weeks later, into autumn.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is endemic and will sting ripening figs, so baiting is essential. Fig rust (Cerotelium fici), which thrives in the warm, humid tropics and subtropics, is the key disease, spotting the leaves and dropping them early; growers control it with copper or mancozeb sprays. Watch also for fig leaf beetle, and for birds and flying foxes on ripe fruit.

faqs.q: Can you grow figs in Brisbane or coastal Queensland?
faqs.a: Yes, but the humid coast is hard on figs: expect fig rust and some fruit splitting, keep the tree open and airy, and be ready to spray for rust. Figs are easier and more reliable in the drier inland and the elevated Granite Belt.

faqs.q: Where in Queensland do figs grow best?
faqs.a: In the drier, cooler country away from the humid coast: the Lockyer Valley west of Brisbane and the elevated Granite Belt around Stanthorpe, where lower humidity means much less disease.

NSW.intro: Growing figs in New South Wales. NSW grows excellent figs in its warm, dry inland, while the humid coast is more of a challenge.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Figs love the hot, dry summers of inland NSW, which is why the best fruit comes from the irrigated Riverina and the Central West rather than the humid coast. Along the coast and through the Sydney basin figs still crop, but wet, humid summers bring fig rust and split fruit, so choose a sunny, open spot and a variety that copes.

sections.heading: Where figs grow in NSW
sections.body: The Riverina around Griffith and Yenda is prime fig country, with growers there specialising in rich Black Genoa figs, and the Central West around Orange has orchards cropping several named varieties. Figs are also a common backyard tree across the Sydney basin and the Hawkesbury and up into the Hunter, where summer humidity is the main limit.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: The hot inland ripens figs from about January to March. In the cooler Central West, orchards take two crops, the first around December and January and the main crop from February into April.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is established across eastern NSW and will use figs as a host, so bait or bag the fruit. Fig rust is mainly a coastal and humid-district problem and far less of an issue in the dry inland. Net against birds, and watch for the fig blister mite, for which there is no registered spray.

faqs.q: When do you harvest figs in NSW?
faqs.a: Roughly January to March in the warm inland. Cooler districts such as the Central West around Orange take two crops, an early one in December and January and the main crop from February to April.

faqs.q: Where are figs grown in NSW?
faqs.a: Mainly the warm, dry inland: the Riverina around Griffith and Yenda and the Central West around Orange. Figs also grow well as backyard trees around Sydney and the Hawkesbury, though the humid coast brings more disease.

VIC.intro: Growing figs in Victoria. Victoria's warm, dry summers and mild winters suit figs well, with the cooler season simply ripening the fruit a little later.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Figs are one of the easier, hardier fruit trees for Victorian gardens, doing well from Melbourne's moderate climate to the warm, dry irrigation districts of the north. The warm inland ripens the best fruit; cooler, wetter southern and coastal districts ripen later and see more fig rust in damp autumns, and frost can set back young trees.

sections.heading: Where figs grow in VIC
sections.body: The Mornington Peninsula south of Melbourne is a noted fig district among its orchards and farm gates, and figs crop well through greater Melbourne and central Victoria around Bendigo. The warm, dry north, including the irrigated Goulburn Valley, ripens figs reliably given summer water.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Victoria's cooler season ripens figs later than the northern states, mainly from February into April, with the warm northern districts a little earlier and a light breba crop in some gardens before that.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly has spread into Victoria and is under area-wide control in fruit-growing districts such as the Goulburn Valley; figs are a host, so bait and monitor. Fig rust is more of a problem in the wetter south than the dry north. Net against birds, and protect young trees from hard frosts.

faqs.q: When do figs ripen in Victoria?
faqs.a: Mainly February to April, later than the warmer northern states because Victoria's cooler season ripens the fruit more slowly. The warm, dry north ripens a little earlier.

faqs.q: Are figs frost hardy in Victoria?
faqs.a: Established figs are hardy and drop their leaves over winter, but young trees and any late fruit can be hurt by hard frost. In cold districts, shelter new trees for their first few winters and give them a warm, sunny spot.
